title: Band Unfolding via the Quadratic Pseudospectrum
authors: Christopher A. Bairnsfather, Ralph M. Kaufmann, Terry A. Loring, Alexander Cerjan
abstract: Band theory provides the foundation for understanding electronic structure in crystalline materials, but its reliance on exact translational symmetry limits its applicability to systems with defects, disorder, incommensurate modulations, or large unit cells. Here, we introduce a band unfolding framework that directly generalizes traditional band theory to systems where exact periodicity is absent, and which remains well-defined for both aperiodic and finite systems. To do so, we employ a pseudospectral approach to identify approximate joint eigenvectors of a system's Hamiltonian and translation operators, thereby yielding an unfolded band structure whose features are directly connected to the manifestation of approximate extended states simultaneously localized in energy and crystalline momentum. To reveal bulk-only spectral phenomena in finite systems, we further show that this pseudospectral framework naturally accommodates additional operators that suppress contributions from boundary-localized states, enabling the systematic isolation of intrinsic bulk behavior. We benchmark the scheme on several representative systems in one and two dimensions, including a Fibonacci chain, where our approach is able to both reveal a dispersive envelope while preserving the underlying hierarchy of spectral gaps. Looking forward, this pseudospectral approach may yield a broad framework for predicting momentum-resolved material responses in aperiodic, disordered, and finite systems where conventional band-theoretic methods are not applicable.
summary: This paper presents a new numerical framework for 'band unfolding' that works for systems without exact periodicity, such as quasicrystals and disordered materials. By using a quadratic pseudospectrum, the method can reconstruct dispersion relations and effectively separate bulk electronic properties from edge states in finite systems.
main_problem: Traditional band theory fails in systems lacking exact translational symmetry, such as disordered, aperiodic, or finite systems, and existing unfolding methods do not guarantee states are simultaneously localized in energy and momentum.
main_result: The authors introduce a quadratic pseudospectrum framework that generalizes band unfolding to aperiodic and finite systems, enabling the reconstruction of dispersion relations and the isolation of bulk properties from boundary effects.
method: A quadratic pseudospectrum approach using a composite operator Q(E, k) to identify approximate joint eigenvectors of the Hamiltonian and translation operators, supplemented by a position-dependent operator to suppress boundary states.
model_or_system: The framework is benchmarked on 1D trimerized lattices, 1D Fibonacci chains (quasiperiodic), and 2D breathing honeycomb lattices (topological/moiré-like).
key_observables: Quadratic gap mu(E, k), quadratic epsilon-pseudospectrum, and momentum-resolved uncertainty (variance and deviation).
important_parameters: Kappa (balance between energy and momentum resolution), kappa_X (spatial localization scale), and hopping amplitudes (v, w, t1, t2).
main_assumptions: The method assumes the existence of almost-commuting operators and is subject to finite-size effects (beading) in real-space implementations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a trimerized lattice schematic and folded/unfolded band heatmaps; Figure 2 compares unfolded spectra with and without boundary suppression; Figure 3 displays the Fibonacci chain dispersion and gap hierarchy; Figure 4 shows reconstructed Dirac points in the breathing honeycomb lattice.
paper_structure: Introduction of the problem (limitations of band theory) -> Mathematical framework (quadratic pseudospectrum and composite operator) -> Implementation of boundary suppression -> Benchmarking on periodic/trimerized systems -> Application to aperiodic (Fibonacci) and 2D (breathing honeycomb) systems -> Error decomposition and uncertainty analysis.
why_interesting: The method provides a robust way to extract momentum-resolved spectral features from complex, non-periodic Hamiltonians, which is highly relevant for analyzing the dynamics and spectra of many-body systems or cold atom lattices with disorder or moiré potentials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Band unfolding of the infinite trimerized lattice with a site-to-site separation of a, hopping amplitudes v = 0.8t and w = 1.2t where t is the energy scale, and κ = t. (a) Schematic of the trimerized lattice. (b) Exact folded band structure of this model. (c) Heatmap of the quadratic gap µ(E,k) in units of t with k chosen in the range of the primitive Brillouin zone k ∈[−π, a, π/a). The analytic solution [42] for the unfolded band structure is superimposed in magenta.

FIG. 2: (a) Approximate unfolded band structure using µ(E,k) of a finite trimerized lattice model with periodic boundary conditions (PBC). The finite lattice has 72 unit cells of 3 sites each, with v = 0.8t and w = 1.2t, and κT = 0.3t. (b) Approximate unfolded band structure using µ(E,k) of the same system, but with open boundary conditions (OBC). (c) Approximate boundary-independent unfolded band structure using µ(E,k,x) with κX = 0.1t with OBC. For x chosen in the lattice’s center, the smoothing function is zero on sites 72 through 144, 1 on sites 1 through 36 and 180 through 216, and smoothly interpolates between 0 and 1 in between, see Supplemental Information [43].

FIG. 4: (a)-(c) Approximate unfolded band structures found using µ(E,k) of the finite breathing honeycomb lattice (shown in the Supplement [43]), over a path in k-space through high-symmetry points of the Brillouin zone. The finite system has 546 total sites and the calculations use κT = 0.1t. Three different realizations of the model are shown, with intra-hexagon couplings t1 = 0.8t and inter-hexagon couplings t2 = 1.2t (a), t1 = t2 = t (b), and t1 = 1.2t, t2 = 0.8t (c).

FIG. 3: Approximate dispersion for an open Fibonacci lattice calculated using the bulk-resolved quadratic gap µ(E,k,x). The chain was formed using 12 recursive substitutions yielding a total system length of 987 sites, in which the coupling coefficients are v = 0.7t and w = 1.0t with t setting the energy scale. Pseudospectral calculations used κT = 0.05t and κX = 0.1(t/a), where a is the site-to-site spacing. Using x in the system’s center and 14 substitutions to obtain a longer chain, the smoothing function selected for the sites 862 through 1722. The predicted mini-gap locations labeled by {p, q} are marked on the right-side of the approximate unfolded band structure [40, 44].
